questions.question: Who was the first man to run a sub-4-minute mile?
questions.explanation: Roger Bannister became the first person to run a mile in under four minutes on May 6, 1954, at Iffley Road Track in Oxford, with a time of 3:59.4. His record was broken just 46 days later by Australian John Landy. Bannister was also a neurologist and later became Master of Pembroke College, Oxford.
questions.memory_hook: Bannister broke the 4-minute mile — then Landy broke his record 46 days later.

questions.question: The 'Protestant Reformation' was triggered by Martin Luther nailing his 95 Theses to a church door in which year?
questions.explanation: Martin Luther nailed his 95 Theses to the door of the Castle Church in Wittenberg on October 31, 1517 (now celebrated as Reformation Day). The theses challenged Catholic Church practices, particularly the sale of indulgences. The printing press allowed Luther's ideas to spread rapidly across Europe.
questions.memory_hook: 1517: Luther nailed it. 95 theses, one door, one revolution.

questions.question: At the 1976 Montreal Olympics, 14-year-old Nadia Comaneci became the first gymnast in Olympic history to receive a perfect score. What made the moment historically awkward?
options: The judges gave her a 10.0, but the scoreboard could only display three digits and showed '1.00' — the technology literally couldn't represent perfection
The judges initially refused to award her a 10.0 and required a vote, delaying the official score for nearly 20 minutes while Comaneci waited on the mat
The crowd booed because Romanian gymnasts were expected to score lower under a new scoring system introduced that year, making a 10.0 mathematically impossible
Comaneci's coach threatened to withdraw the entire Romanian team unless the judges upgraded her score from 9.95 to 10.0, triggering an international diplomatic dispute
questions.answer: The judges gave her a 10.0, but the scoreboard could only display three digits and showed '1.00' — the technology literally couldn't represent perfection
questions.explanation: The Montreal scoreboard was designed to show scores up to 9.99 — nobody had ever anticipated a perfect 10. When Comaneci earned it on the uneven bars, the scoreboard displayed '1.00.' The announcer had to clarify to a confused audience. Comaneci went on to earn seven perfect 10s at those Games, winning three gold medals. She was coached by Béla Károlyi, who later coached the US Olympic gymnastics team. Her performance transformed gymnastics into a widely watched sport and made Romania internationally famous.
questions.memory_hook: The 1976 scoreboard showed '1.00' when Comaneci earned the first perfect 10 — it was designed before anyone expected perfection. She scored seven 10s at those Games, winning three golds at 14.

questions.question: Which swimmer won 8 gold medals at the 2008 Beijing Olympics — the most by any athlete at a single Games — breaking Mark Spitz's 1972 record of 7?
questions.explanation: Michael Phelps won 8 gold medals at the 2008 Beijing Olympics, surpassing Mark Spitz's 1972 record of 7. Phelps went on to become the most decorated Olympian of all time with 23 gold medals and 28 total medals across four Olympic Games.
questions.memory_hook: Phelps 2008 Beijing = 8 golds. Most decorated Olympian ever.

questions.question: Which band wrote and performed 'Comfortably Numb'?
questions.explanation: 'Comfortably Numb' appears on Pink Floyd's concept album 'The Wall' (1979). The song features two solos by guitarist David Gilmour, with the outro solo widely considered one of the greatest guitar solos of all time. Roger Waters wrote the lyrics; Gilmour wrote the music.
questions.memory_hook: 'Comfortably Numb' — Pink Floyd's Wall, Gilmour's guitar solo, Waters' words.

questions.question: Which 1999 science fiction film starring Keanu Reeves depicts a dystopian future where humans live in a simulated reality controlled by machines?
questions.explanation: The Matrix (1999), directed by the Wachowskis, became a landmark in science fiction cinema. It popularized 'bullet time' photography, grossed over $460 million worldwide, and spawned a franchise while introducing philosophical concepts about simulated reality to mainstream audiences.
questions.memory_hook: The Matrix (1999) = red pill or blue pill. 'What is real?'

questions.question: David Bowie created the alter ego 'Ziggy Stardust' in 1972 — a bisexual alien rock star — then famously 'killed' the character on stage in 1973. Why is this moment significant in rock history?
options: Bowie used Ziggy Stardust to smuggle coded anti-apartheid messages into his music during a 1972 South African tour, and 'killing' the character allowed him to speak more openly about political activism

It was one of rock's first deliberate examples of performance art as celebrity persona — Bowie created, performed, and then consciously destroyed a character, treating pop music as theater and blurring the line between artist and identity
Ziggy Stardust was originally conceived as a film character, and 'killing' him on stage in 1973 was a contractual requirement before the movie adaptation could proceed
Bowie retired Ziggy after learning that fans in several countries had begun religious cults around the character, believing Ziggy was a real alien messenger
questions.answer: It was one of rock's first deliberate examples of performance art as celebrity persona — Bowie created, performed, and then consciously destroyed a character, treating pop music as theater and blurring the line between artist and identity
questions.explanation: At the Hammersmith Odeon on July 3, 1973, Bowie told the audience 'not only is it the last show of the tour, but it's the last show we'll ever do' — shocking the Spiders from Mars band members who had no warning. The announcement ended Ziggy and the Spiders simultaneously. Bowie's use of constructed personas (Thin White Duke, Aladdin Sane, Major Tom) throughout his career influenced everyone from Lady Gaga to Kanye West. The 1972 Ziggy Stardust album combined glam rock, androgyny, and science fiction mythology in a way that permanently altered rock's relationship to theatricality.
questions.memory_hook: Bowie killed Ziggy Stardust on stage July 3, 1973 without telling his band — they learned their employment was over in the same moment as the audience. The deliberate creation and destruction of a persona treated pop music as theater; it's still rock's most influential act of performance art.

questions.question: The 'Battle of Thermopylae' is famous for which force's last stand?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Thermopylae (480 BC) saw approximately 300 Spartans (and about 700 Thespians) under King Leonidas hold off a Persian army estimated in the hundreds of thousands for three days. Their sacrifice allowed the Greek forces to regroup and eventually defeat the Persians at Salamis.
questions.memory_hook: 300 Spartans vs. Persia — 'Tonight we dine in hell!' Three days, one narrow pass.

questions.question: Indonesia has the most islands of any country. Approximately how many does it have?
questions.explanation: Indonesia consists of approximately 17,000 islands, of which about 6,000 are inhabited. It is the world's largest archipelago nation. Major islands include Java, Sumatra, Borneo (shared with Malaysia and Brunei), Sulawesi, and half of New Guinea. With 277 million people, it is the world's fourth most populous country.
questions.memory_hook: Indonesia = 17,000 islands. About 6,000 inhabited. It's the world's largest archipelago — imagine running a country spread across the ocean over an area wider than the continental US.

questions.question: Karl Jansky's accidental discovery in 1932 gave birth to an entirely new branch of astronomy. What did he find, and what has radio astronomy revealed that optical telescopes cannot?
options: Jansky discovered that stars emit radio waves in addition to light, proving that visible light represents only a tiny fraction of the electromagnetic spectrum that stars produce

Jansky discovered that radio interference in telephone circuits was coming from the center of the Milky Way — founding radio astronomy and enabling discoveries including pulsars, quasars, the CMB, and the first images of black holes

Jansky discovered that the universe is transparent to radio waves — that they can travel through dense gas clouds that block visible light — immediately revealing the galactic center that optical astronomers had never seen

Jansky discovered artificial radio signals from the direction of Andromeda galaxy in 1932, which turned out to be the first evidence of the galactic hiss that was later renamed the Cosmic Microwave Background
questions.answer: Jansky discovered that radio interference in telephone circuits was coming from the center of the Milky Way — founding radio astronomy and enabling discoveries including pulsars, quasars, the CMB, and the first images of black holes
questions.explanation: Jansky was a Bell Labs engineer trying to identify sources of telephone static. He identified three sources: nearby thunderstorms, distant thunderstorms, and a mystery hiss that moved across the sky on a 23.9-hour cycle — Earth's rotation relative to the stars. He correctly identified it as radio emission from the Milky Way's center. Radio astronomy subsequently revealed: the cosmic microwave background (accidental discovery, 1965), pulsars (1967), quasars, the structure of the Milky Way (impossible optically due to dust), and produced the Event Horizon Telescope's black hole images. Most of modern cosmology depends on radio observations.
questions.memory_hook: Jansky was trying to fix telephone interference in 1932 and traced a mystery hiss to the Milky Way's center. Radio astronomy now does what optical telescopes cannot — see through dust clouds, map our galaxy's spiral arms, image black holes, and detect the Big Bang's afterglow. Most of modern cosmology is radio astronomy.

questions.question: The Atlas Mountains, a range spanning approximately 2,500 km, run across which three North African countries?
questions.explanation: The Atlas Mountains run across Morocco, Algeria, and Tunisia from the Atlantic coast near Agadir to northeastern Tunisia. Toubkal in Morocco (4,167 m) is the highest peak. The Atlas range forms a geographic barrier between the Mediterranean and Atlantic coasts (relatively fertile) and the Sahara Desert to the south. The Berber (Amazigh) people have inhabited the Atlas for thousands of years. The mountains are named after the Titan Atlas from Greek mythology.
questions.memory_hook: Atlas Mountains: Morocco + Algeria + Tunisia, ~2,500 km. Toubkal (4,167m) = highest peak. Separates Mediterranean coast from Sahara. Home of Berber/Amazigh people for millennia. Named after the Greek Titan Atlas, who holds up the sky — fitting for a range that holds back the desert.

questions.question: What is the term for different forms of the same chemical element that have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons in their nuclei?
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element that have different numbers of neutrons, giving them different atomic masses. Carbon-12 (6 protons, 6 neutrons) and carbon-14 (6 protons, 8 neutrons) are isotopes of carbon. Most isotopes are stable, but some are radioactive — their nuclei decay over time. Carbon-14's radioactive decay is used in radiocarbon dating. Uranium-235 and uranium-238 are isotopes; U-235 is fissile (used in reactors and bombs) while U-238 is not.
questions.memory_hook: Isotopes = same element, different neutron counts, different mass. Carbon-12 vs Carbon-14 — same chemistry, but C-14 is radioactive. C-14 dating works because C-14's half-life (5,730 years) is predictable. U-235 is fissile (used in reactors); U-238 is not. Same element, completely different behavior.
